figures.gloss: The transcendent, genderless supreme creator who shaped the universe and subsequently withdrew, delegating the active management of reality to the pantheons of lesser gods (the winti). Anana is rarely worshipped directly but is invoked as the ultimate source of life.
figures.facet: Anana is entirely removed from the transactional, highly negotiated possession rituals that define Winti practice; one does not become possessed by Anana, only by their subordinates.
figures.provenance: C.J. Wooding, 'Winti: Een Afroamerikaanse godsdienst in Suriname' (1972).

figures.gloss: The preeminent Earth spirit (Grong-winti) and maternal head of the Winti pantheon. She governs the soil, market trade, illness, and communal harmony. Mama Aisa demands strict cleanliness and respect for the earth, rewarding her devotees with health and prosperity.
figures.facet: She is both indigenous to Suriname's soil and profoundly African, acting as the primary maternal anchor who roots the uprooted enslaved population in a new, sacred American geography.
figures.provenance: Charles J. Wooding, 'Evolving Culture: A Cross-Cultural Study of Suriname, West Africa and the Caribbean' (1981).

figures.gloss: Fierce, airborne warrior spirits tracing their lineage to Akan and enslaved Coromantee rebels. Associated with thunder, the eagle, iron, and potent herbal magic (obia), they are invoked for courage, deep healing, and exorcism.
figures.facet: They are the explicitly militant, anti-colonial forces of Winti, preserving the martial resistance and aggressive invulnerability of maroon rebels as a permanent, accessible spiritual frequency.
figures.provenance: H.U.E. Thoden van Velzen & W. van Wetering, 'In the Shadow of the Oracle' (1988).

figures.gloss: Diminutive, notoriously capricious spirits of the deep forest (Busi-winti). Highly territorial and sexually aggressive, they command immense magical power but are easily offended, often demanding complex appeasement before logging or hunting.
figures.facet: They embody the terrifying, untamed, and morally ambiguous power of the South American jungle, standing in stark contrast to the ordered communal spaces governed by Aisa.
figures.provenance: Wilhelmina van Wetering, 'Demonology and the Classic Witchcraft Syndrome in Suriname' (1973).

figures.gloss: The ragged, broom-wielding spirit of the crossroads and thresholds. Leba must be saluted and propitiated first in any ritual, as he sweeps away negative forces and opens the spiritual gateways for other winti to arrive.
figures.facet: Unlike his regal West African counterparts, Suriname's Leba is often visualized as a beggar in tattered clothes, reflecting the marginalized, impoverished reality of the enslaved who recreated him.
figures.provenance: C.J. Wooding, 'Winti: Een Afroamerikaanse godsdienst in Suriname' (1972).

figures.gloss: The immortal, bifurcated human soul, fundamentally shaped by Akan cosmology. It consists of a male and female half, closely bound to the day of the week a person was born, requiring regular spiritual cleansing (wasi) to maintain psychological balance.
figures.facet: It asserts that the individual is not a monolithic self but a precarious partnership of divine energies that can literally fall out of alignment, causing physical and mental illness if neglected.
figures.provenance: H.J.M. Stephen, 'Winti, Afro-Surinaamse religie en magische rituelen' (1983).

figures.gloss: The mesmerizing, comb-holding queen of the water spirits (Watra-winti). She governs rivers and the sea, granting immense wealth and clairvoyance to her favorites, but punishing infidelity or broken vows with severe misfortune or drowning.
figures.facet: She fuses the ancient African Mami Wata complex with indigenous Amerindian river mythology, demanding a terrifying exclusivity that often isolates her mediums from normal social bonds.
figures.provenance: Alex van Stipriaan, 'Watramama/Mami Wata: Three centuries of creolization of a water spirit in West Africa, Suriname and Europe' (2005).

figures.gloss: The collective spirits of the dead, specifically the enslaved ancestors. They are venerated at the Kabra-neti (ancestor night) and provide foundational guidance, protection, and identity to the living family lines (lo).
figures.facet: They are explicitly historical, trauma-bearing ancestors; they are not just the dead, but the victims and survivors of the Middle Passage and plantation slavery, whose specific suffering must be ritually acknowledged to heal the living.
figures.provenance: Gloria Wekker, 'The Politics of Passion: Women's Sexual Culture in the Afro-Surinamese Diaspora' (2006).

figures.gloss: The Fodu (Papa) winti — sacred serpent spirits of Dahomean (vodun) descent, most closely tied to the boa (daguwe), associated with the earth, calm dignity, and undivided attention from their mediums.
figures.facet: Papa Winti's serpent form carries the Dan/Ayida-Wedo complex intact across the Middle Passage into a new Guianese pantheon, standing apart from Winti's more volatile forest and storm spirits by demanding a slow, deliberate mode of service.
figures.provenance: Charles J. Wooding, 'Winti: Een Afroamerikaanse godsdienst in Suriname' (1972); the tradition's own period note.

figures.gloss: A small, half-wood, half-flesh spirit-double purchased from a sorcerer to bring its owner sudden wealth; feared above nearly every other figure in Surinamese lore because it eventually turns on the buyer's own descendants to collect its price.
figures.facet: Bakru is Winti's made spirit, not a natural one — its danger is contractual rather than territorial, a warning built into the tradition against wealth acquired outside the reciprocal economy the winti otherwise require.
figures.provenance: Charles J. Wooding's fieldwork; H.U.E. Thoden van Velzen & W. van Wetering's Surinamese ethnography.

figures.gloss: The spirit of an ordinary dead person — as distinct from the collectively venerated Kabra — capable of lingering to haunt the living or being deliberately sent against an enemy, and requiring its own rites to be laid to rest.
figures.facet: Yorka is the working, unsettled counterpart to the elevated Kabra: where the Kabra are honored ancestors, the yorka is the raw, morally ambiguous fact of an individual death not yet resolved.
figures.provenance: Gloria Wekker, 'The Politics of Passion' (2006); H.J.M. Stephen's ethnography of Winti ritual practice.

figures.gloss: The 'Indian' winti — spirits of Suriname's indigenous peoples, adopted into the Afro-Surinamese pantheon and worked with tobacco offerings and their own distinct rhythms and songs, separate from the African-derived classes of spirit.
figures.facet: Their presence in the pantheon records an actual Afro-Indigenous religious exchange in the Guianese interior, not a borrowed motif — Winti's other spirit-classes are African or creole in origin, making the Ingi winti the one class indigenous to Suriname itself.
figures.provenance: Charles J. Wooding's pantheon classification, 'Winti' (1972) and 'Evolving Culture' (1981).

figures.gloss: The craft, charged substances, and specialist knowledge of spiritual medicine — worked by the obiaman or obiawoman — used for protection, healing, and, in its darker applications, harm; named directly in Kromanti practice as their potent herbal magic.
figures.facet: Obia is Winti's whole operative technology rather than a single spirit or rite: the working layer that turns the pantheon's blessings and dangers into applied, purchasable, teachable skill.
figures.provenance: H.U.E. Thoden van Velzen & W. van Wetering, 'In the Shadow of the Oracle' (1988); the winti-kromanti entry's own citation of obia.

figures.gloss: The night-long ceremony of drumming, song, and dance at which mediums are mounted by their winti, one spirit-class in turn according to strict drum rhythms and songs proper to each — the central and virtually only public rite of Winti religion.
figures.facet: Every named spirit in the pantheon is, in practice, known through the wintiprey: not one rite among several but the single ritual technology through which the entire system becomes visible and operative.
figures.provenance: H.U.E. Thoden van Velzen & W. van Wetering's ethnography of Surinamese Winti ceremonies; Charles J. Wooding (1972).

figures.gloss: Inherited dietary and behavioral taboos owed to one's personal and lineage winti — foods, animals, or acts that must be avoided on pain of illness or the spirit's displeasure — the term itself a Sephardic Portuguese-Jewish loanword (trefu/treife) into Sranan Tongo.
figures.facet: Trefu records, in its very vocabulary, Suriname's improbable multi-religious contact history: a Jewish dietary-law term became the operative word for African-derived spirit obligation.
figures.provenance: H.J.M. Stephen, 'Winti, Afro-Surinaamse religie en magische rituelen' (1983); Gloria Wekker's ethnography.

edges.note: Intra-tradition bond: The withdrawn supreme creator and the active Earth Mother to whom direct governance of humanity is delegated.
Intra-tradition bond: The ragged opener of the crossroads who must sweep the path before the regal Earth Mother can arrive.
Intra-tradition bond: The elevated, sky-bound martial discipline of the Kromanti contrasted with the chaotic, earth-bound unpredictability of the Apuku.
Intra-tradition bond: The individual soul (Kra) that, upon proper rites at death, is elevated to join the collective ancestral pool (Kabra).
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Direct linguistic and theological continuity from the Akan supreme sky god to the Surinamese Winti creator.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The transcendent, ultimately unknowable supreme being of Afro-diasporic cosmologies who acts through lesser spirits.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The Akan earth goddess retained and adapted as the head of the Surinamese terrestrial pantheon.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The sacred, maternal embodiment of the South American earth, requiring propitiation before any disruption of the soil.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The fiery, airborne, thunder-wielding spirit who represents explosive justice and martial power.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Fierce spirits of iron, war, and aggressive healing through radical action.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Direct descent of the Dahomeyan/Fon trickster and opener of the crossroads.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The wild, territorial lord of the deep forest who exists completely outside human communal order.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The capricious, easily offended master of the woods who controls access to the forest's bounty.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A delicate, autonomous inner soul whose misalignment or defilement is the primary cause of physical illness.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The terrifying, demanding female sovereign of the waters who guards immense wealth and punishes human transgression.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The Akan concept of venerated, active ancestors mapping directly onto the Afro-Surinamese Kabra.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Reincarnated ancestral presences who actively return to guide, protect, and enforce lineage law among the living.
Both are Afro-Caribbean mermaid-spirits who claim and transform those they take beneath the water — a shared regional water-woman complex, not a single traceable line of descent.
direct Dahomean (Fon) descent — the Fodu serpent complex carried into Suriname's Winti pantheon.
parallel diaspora descendant of the same Dahomean serpent complex, developed independently in Haiti.
Intra-tradition: the made, purchased spirit-double against the natural, territorial forest spirit — two very different sources of Winti's forest-linked danger.
Intra-tradition: the unsettled, individual dead against the collectively venerated, elevated ancestors.
Intra-tradition: the yorka is what a kra becomes when its death has not been properly resolved by rite.
Intra-tradition: the Ingi winti descend at the wintiprey to their own tobacco offerings and distinct rhythms.
Intra-tradition: obia is named directly in the Kromanti's own gloss as their potent herbal magic.
obia and obeah are cognate terms for charged magical craft and substance, both rooted in West African vocabulary carried into separate Caribbean and Guianese diasporas.
Intra-tradition: trefu obligations are owed specifically to one's personal kra and its governing winti.

dossier.essence: An Afro-Surinamese religion developed by enslaved West and Central Africans, especially Akan, Fon, and Kongo peoples, and their Maroon and Creole descendants in Suriname. It centers on a withdrawn creator, Anana, and a large hierarchy of nature- and ancestor-spirits (winti, literally 'winds') who engage the living through possession. Long suppressed and criminalized under colonial rule, it remains widely practiced in Suriname and the Surinamese-Dutch diaspora.
dossier.cosmos: Anana Keduaman Keduampon is the transcendent, genderless creator who withdrew and delegated the world to the winti, organized by domain: earth (Aisa and the Grong-winti), water (Watramama and the Watra-winti), forest (Apuku and the Busi-winti), and the fierce Akan-derived sky-warriors (Kromanti). The human person is not unitary: an immortal soul (kra), tied to the day of one's birth, must be kept in balance through cleansing; the dead join the ancestral collective (kabra), whose specific suffering under slavery is ritually honored.
dossier.practice: Practice centers on the winti-prei ('spirit-play'), where drumming, song, and dance call particular winti to possess devotees for healing, divination, and guidance, mediated by ritual specialists (bonuman, obiaman) and by herbal-magical knowledge (obia). Ritual purity, offerings, ancestor veneration (as at Kabra-neti), and spiritual bathing (wasi) to keep the soul aligned are central, and Leba, the ragged opener of the crossroads, is saluted first.
dossier.canon: An oral tradition with no scripture, carried in songs, ritual languages (such as Kromanti/Kumanti), proverbs, and initiatory practice; documented in the ethnography of C. J. Wooding, H. J. M. Stephen, and others.
dossier.reflects: It foregrounds religion as the reconstitution of a shattered people, a spirituality that re-roots the uprooted in new soil while ritually carrying the specific historical wound of enslavement.
sources: C. J. Wooding, 'Winti: Een Afroamerikaanse godsdienst in Suriname' (1972)
H. J. M. Stephen, 'Winti: Afro-Surinaamse religie en magische rituelen' (1983)
H. U. E. Thoden van Velzen & W. van Wetering, 'In the Shadow of the Oracle: Religion as Politics in a Suriname Maroon Society' (1988)
